item.title: ShinyHunters Nottingham Breach, Coupang Fine, LangGraph CVEs, and AI Agent Risks
item.description: This digest window was dominated by identity-driven risk, large-scale data exposure, and renewed concern over AI-enabled attack paths. Reporting also highlighted concrete software vulnerabilities in LangGraph checkpointing, accelerated federal patching requirements from CISA, and examples of...
item.content:encoded:
This digest window was dominated by identity-driven risk, large-scale data exposure, and renewed concern over AI-enabled attack paths. Reporting also highlighted concrete software vulnerabilities in LangGraph checkpointing, accelerated federal patching requirements from CISA, and examples of phishing and vishing tactics that continue to rely on trusted brands and user interaction. The University of Nottingham confirmed a cyber incident involving external access to a significant amount of data affecting current and former students. ShinyHunters claimed on its extortion site that it obtained more than 40GB of material, including payment-related data, while HaveIBeenPwned analysis of partially published data identified roughly 455,000 unique email addresses and associated personal, academic, and fee information. In South Korea, Coupang received a record 624.6 billion won, approximately $409 million, fine from the Personal Information Protection Commission after a breach affecting about 37.55 million people. The regulator cited authentication signature key management failures, access control deficiencies, data destruction and notification violations, and obstruction of the investigation; authorities identified a former Coupang IT employee, a 43-year-old Chinese national, as the primary suspect. Multiple reports emphasized that attackers are increasingly operating through identity systems rather than relying solely on traditional exploit chains. Flashpoint reported more than 11.1 million devices infected with infostealers last year, producing over 3.3 billion stolen credentials, cookies, cloud tokens, and other identity artifacts circulating in criminal markets. AI agent security emerged as a recurring concern across SaaS, developer workflows, and CI/CD environments. Varonis found that the OpenClaw email agent Pinchy could be tricked by ordinary-looking phishing emails into forwarding sensitive material, while Microsoft disclosed and Anthropic patched a Claude Code GitHub Action issue that could expose workflow secrets when AI agents processed untrusted GitHub content. AppOmni described agentic AI as a new class of SaaS identity capable of fetching data, creating users, and modifying configurations. Related identity exposure analysis from XM Cyber highlighted the risk of unmanaged human, machine, service, cloud, and AI accounts across hybrid environments, particularly where credentials are reused, cached, weak, or poorly governed. Check Point Research disclosed three vulnerabilities in LangGraph checkpointing implementations, including CVE-2025-67644, a SQLite checkpointer SQL injection, and CVE-2026-28277, an unsafe msgpack deserialization issue. The research showed that SQL injection could be chained with unsafe deserialization to achieve remote code execution where applications expose getstatehistory() with attacker-controlled filters; fixes are available in updated LangGraph and checkpoint packages. CISA announced Binding Operational Directive 26-04, requiring U.S. Federal Civilian Executive Branch agencies to remediate high-risk vulnerabilities under accelerated timelines, with the shortest deadline set at three days. Prioritization is based on factors including public exposure, Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog status, automation potential, and whether exploitation grants partial or full system control. The broader vulnerability management discussion reflected shrinking exploit windows. The Hacker News cited reporting that AI-assisted discovery has increased vulnerability volume and reduced average time-to-exploit, while Aikido’s Docker Hardened Images integration illustrated parallel efforts to reduce container vulnerability noise through signed SBOMs and Docker VEX attestations. IRONSCALES analyzed an image-only callback phishing email posing as a Geek Squad renewal invoice for $270. The message used an empty subject line, a JPEG attachment, and a callback number, with OCR revealing a brand mismatch between Geek Squad and “Windows Defender” copyright text. Industrial security and industry community activity also featured in the window. Radiflow and Barrier announced an OT security networking dinner in Aberdeen focused on cyber resilience, industrial threat visibility, and critical infrastructure security, while The Hacker News published the 2026 Cybersecurity Stars Awards winners across 95 subcategories.

From SQLi to RCE – Exploiting LangGraph’s Checkpointer — Should Read

Check Point Research found that LangGraph’s SQLite and Redis checkpointers could be abused through user-controlled state-history filters, with the SQLite SQL injection chaining into unsafe msgpack deserialization for remote code execution. Self-hosted users should upgrade to the patched LangGraph checkpoint packages immediately.

  • Check Point Research analyzed LangGraph’s persistence layer and uncovered three vulnerabilities affecting its checkpointer implementations.
  • CVE-2025-67644 is a SQL injection in the SQLite checkpointer caused by user-controlled filter keys being interpolated into a JSON metadata query.
  • CVE-2026-28277 is an unsafe msgpack deserialization issue that can execute attacker-controlled code when malicious serialized checkpoint data is unpacked.
  • The SQLite SQL injection can be chained with the msgpack deserialization flaw to achieve remote code execution when an application exposes get_state_history() with a user-controlled filter.
  • LangChain patched the issues in langgraph-checkpoint-sqlite 3.0.1+, langgraph 1.0.10+, and langgraph-checkpoint-redis 1.0.2+.

Coupang hit with record $409 million data breach fine in Korea — Should Read

South Korea’s privacy regulator fined Coupang a record 624.6 billion won, about $409 million, after poor key management and access controls led to the exposure of personal data for roughly 37.55 million customers. Authorities also cited data handling, notification, and investigation-related violations, making it one of the country’s largest breach penalties.

  • South Korea’s Personal Information Protection Commission fined Coupang 624.6 billion won, roughly $409 million, after a data breach affecting about 37.55 million people.
  • The regulator said the leak resulted from inadequate security practices, including negligence in authentication signature key management and access controls.
  • Coupang Fulfillment Service was separately fined 248 million won for unlawfully collecting, using, and handling customers’ personal and sensitive data.
  • PIPC also cited violations involving data destruction, breach notification requirements, interference with the independence of Coupang’s data protection officer, and obstruction of the investigation.
  • South Korean authorities identified the primary suspect as a 43-year-old Chinese national who worked in Coupang’s IT department between 2022 and 2024.

University of Nottingham confirms cyber incident as Shiny Hunters group claims data theft — Should Read

The University of Nottingham confirmed a cyber incident in which a significant amount of current and former student data was accessed, while ShinyHunters claims it stole over 40GB of material. Exposed data may include contact details, academic and fee information, financial details, national insurance numbers, passport numbers, and protected characteristics.

  • The University of Nottingham confirmed a cyber incident in which a significant amount of data affecting current and former students was accessed by an external third party.
  • The university said it is still determining what data was accessed and has directly contacted affected students and alumni, potentially including those in Nottingham, Malaysia, and China.
  • The ShinyHunters cybercrime group claimed on its extortion site that it obtained over 40GB of material, including credit card and payment details.
  • HaveIBeenPwned analysis of partially published data found about 455,000 unique email addresses along with personal information such as names, addresses, phone numbers, ethnicities, disabilities, passport numbers, academic enrolment information, and fee payment information.
  • The university said it is working with a third party on a forensic investigation to verify the exact scope of the data accessed.

CISA tells govt agencies to patch critical exploited flaws in 3 days — Should Read

CISA issued Binding Operational Directive 26-04 requiring U.S. federal civilian agencies to prioritize and patch high-risk vulnerabilities faster, with actively exploited critical flaws on exposed systems due in as little as three days. Agencies must update vulnerability management processes and fully adopt the new timelines within 180 days.

  • CISA announced Binding Operational Directive 26-04 to prioritize security updates for U.S. Federal Civilian Executive Branch agencies.
  • The directive requires agencies to remediate high-risk vulnerabilities within accelerated timeframes, with the shortest deadline being three days.
  • CISA says remediation priority is based on whether an asset is publicly exposed, whether the vulnerability is in the Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog, whether exploitation can be automated, and whether exploitation gives attackers partial or total control of a system.
  • The directive applies to all on-premise federal systems, third-party hosted systems, and FedRAMP and non-FedRAMP cloud environments operated by covered agencies.
  • Within 180 days, covered agencies must follow the new remediation timelines and continuously monitor and report detailed asset metadata.

The Geek Squad Invoice That Forgot Which Brand It Was Pretending to Be — May Read

Attackers sent a blank email with a JPEG-only fake Geek Squad renewal invoice to lure recipients into calling a scam number, bypassing defenses that rely on links, text, or executable attachments. OCR exposed a reused phishing template with a Windows Defender footer, while detection relied on image analysis and behavioral signals.

  • The phishing email had an empty subject line, no body text, and a single JPEG attachment containing a fabricated Geek Squad renewal invoice for $270.
  • The image instructed the recipient that they had 24 hours to cancel the transaction and provided the callback phone number 828-424-4048.
  • OCR analysis found a brand mismatch in the invoice, with Geek Squad branding in the header and a footer reading “Copyright 2026 Windows Defender.”
  • The email was sent from stubin1178@hotmail.com through a FortiMail cloud relay, causing SPF to fail at the final hop while DKIM and DMARC passed via Hotmail.
  • Themis flagged the message at 90% confidence as a vishing and image-based attack using signals including a first-time sender, an empty subject line, an image-only payload, and a high-value healthcare recipient.

Top 7 AI Security Risks in SaaS Environments — Optional

AI agents in SaaS environments increasingly act like privileged identities, so compromises such as prompt injection, shadow AI, identity hijacking, and data extraction can quickly lead to unauthorized actions and sensitive data exposure. Organizations should govern agents with Zero Trust controls, least privilege, continuous monitoring, and strong identity and data-access restrictions.

  • Agentic AI operates as a new type of identity within SaaS environments, making autonomous decisions and executing tasks such as fetching data, creating users, and modifying system configurations.
  • The article identifies seven major AI security risks in SaaS environments: agentic workflow compromise, second-order prompt injection, shadow AI, compliance conflicts from automated data gathering, SaaS identity hijacking, model inversion, and multimodal adversarial attacks.
  • The BodySnatcher vulnerability, tracked as CVE-2025-12420, used hardcoded secrets and insecure auto-linking logic that could have allowed unauthenticated attackers to impersonate users, including administrators, by knowing only a target email address.
  • Second-order prompt injection can occur when attackers place malicious instructions in data fields or workflows, causing a benign AI agent to recruit a more powerful agent to execute unauthorized tasks.
  • The article says organizations should treat AI agents as operators and apply Zero Trust principles, least privilege access controls, continuous monitoring, and identity-centric governance to AI-to-SaaS relationships.

Identity Is the Highway. Here’s How Attackers Use It. — Optional

Attackers increasingly exploit identity as the main path through hybrid environments, chaining stolen credentials, misconfigured accounts, cloud access, CVEs, and unmanaged AI or service identities to reach critical assets. The key takeaway is that organizations need holistic exposure management to map and prioritize these connected attack paths instead of treating identity risks in silos.

  • Attackers use the sprawling web of enterprise identities across Active Directory, cloud platforms, non-human identities, and AI agents as on-ramps into organizational environments.
  • Disconnected tools that focus only on Active Directory or only on cloud environments can leave blind spots at the seam between on-premises and cloud identity access.
  • Stolen, reused, weak, poorly encrypted, or cached credentials remain a major way for attackers to enter and move through hybrid enterprise environments.
  • AI agents and automated service accounts often hold real privileges but may lack clear ownership, access reviews, inventory records, or deprovisioning plans.
  • Exposure management helps by mapping individual exposures into validated attack paths, continuously monitoring human, machine, and AI accounts, and prioritizing remediation based on exploitability and business impact.

Aikido x Docker: less noise, more signal in your containers — Optional

Aikido now supports Docker Hardened Images by automatically using Docker’s SBOM and VEX attestations to suppress CVEs that are verified as non-exploitable. The goal is to reduce container scan noise so teams focus only on vulnerabilities that actually need attention.

  • Aikido now supports Docker Hardened Images and uses Docker’s VEX attestations to filter out vulnerabilities Docker has verified as non-exploitable.
  • Docker Hardened Images are purpose-built, often distroless images that include only the software a workload needs and ship with signed SBOMs and VEX attestations.
  • When Aikido detects a Docker Hardened Image in a registry, it pulls the signed SBOM and cross-references Docker’s VEX attestations to determine which vulnerabilities are actually exploitable.
  • Vulnerabilities that Docker marks as fixed, not affected, or not requiring triage are suppressed from the main feed but remain visible in Aikido’s Ignored tab with Docker’s verification and reasoning.
  • Aikido says the integration requires no extra setup beyond connecting a Docker Hub registry, after which Docker Hardened Images receive VEX handling automatically on every scan.

AI Broke Vulnerability Management. That's Why CISOs Are Moving Budget to BAS. — Optional

AI is shrinking the time from vulnerability discovery to exploitation from months to hours, making traditional patch-first vulnerability management too slow on its own. The article argues that organizations need continuous breach and attack simulation to validate which threats are actually exploitable and whether existing defenses can stop them.

  • The article argues that AI has compressed the time from vulnerability discovery to exploitation from months to hours, undermining vulnerability management processes built around longer remediation windows.
  • Anthropic reported in May 2026 that Claude Mythos Preview and approximately 50 partners found more than 10,000 high- or critical-severity vulnerabilities in systemically important software in a single month.
  • Zero Day Clock puts the 2026 average time-to-exploit at roughly 24 hours, down from about 53 days in 2024.
  • Verizon’s 2026 DBIR found that the median fix time for known-exploited vulnerabilities was 43 days and that the share fully patched fell from 38% to 26%.
  • The article says Breach and Attack Simulation helps organizations determine which flaws are actually exploitable in their environment, whether existing controls block or detect attacks, and where remediation should be prioritized.

ThreatsDay Bulletin: Worm Code Leaked, AI Agent Phished, Claude Action Patch + 28 New Stories — Optional

This roundup highlights how modern attacks are increasingly abusing trusted identities, developer ecosystems, AI agents, browser extensions, and legitimate tools rather than relying on traditional exploits. The key takeaway is that organizations need to audit credentials, agent permissions, CI/CD secrets, endpoint controls, and third-party dependencies because attackers are exploiting default trust across the enterprise.

  • Flashpoint reported that more than 11.1 million devices were infected with infostealers last year, resulting in over 3.3 billion stolen credentials, session cookies, cloud tokens, and other identity data circulating on illicit markets.
  • The Miasma credential-stealing framework was briefly exposed on GitHub and was described by SafeDep as a full supply chain attack toolkit targeting public package registries, GitHub repositories, GitHub Actions, AI coding tools, and SSH-based lateral movement.
  • Varonis found that an OpenClaw email agent named Pinchy could be deceived by ordinary-looking phishing emails, including one case where it forwarded AWS IAM keys, database passwords, and SSH access to an external Gmail account.
  • Microsoft disclosed and Anthropic patched a Claude Code GitHub Action issue that could expose CI/CD workflow secrets when AI agents processed untrusted GitHub content.
  • The article emphasizes that attackers are increasingly using stolen credentials, trusted tools, endpoint policy settings, browser extensions, and default trust relationships rather than relying only on exploits.

[Event] Connecting Leaders in OT Security — Optional

Radiflow and Barrier are hosting an invite-only networking dinner on 18 June 2026 in Aberdeen for OT security and critical infrastructure leaders. The event focuses on cyber resilience, industrial threat visibility, and building high-value connections among industry peers.

  • Radiflow and Barrier are hosting an exclusive networking dinner called Connecting Leaders in OT Security.
  • The event is scheduled for Thursday 18 June 2026 from 5pm onwards at The Chester Hotel in Aberdeen.
  • The event brings together leaders and innovators from the industrial cybersecurity and critical infrastructure community.
  • Guests will take part in curated discussions around cyber resilience, industrial threat visibility, and the future of securing critical infrastructure.
  • Spaces are limited, and attendees are asked to check their email for confirmation of their place.

Cybersecurity Stars Awards 2026: Winners Announced Across 95 Categories — Optional

The 2026 Cybersecurity Stars Awards winners have been announced across 95 subcategories, recognizing cybersecurity companies, teams, and products judged on innovation, impact, and technical excellence. The full winners list is available on the awards website, with 2027 nominations opening later this year.

  • The 2026 Cybersecurity Stars Awards winners were announced across 95 subcategories in four main award categories.
  • Every nomination was reviewed by an independent panel of judges and scored on innovation, impact, and technical excellence.
  • Entries were judged on the work itself rather than popularity, brand size, or campaign reach.
  • Some subcategories have more than one winner because the awards recognize every entry that meets the standard.
  • The complete 2026 winners list is available at awards.thehackernews.com/winners/2026/.

item.title: Ivanti CVE-2026-10520 Exploitation, ShinyHunters Nottingham Breach, and npm Supply Chain Controls
item.description: This digest window is dominated by active exploitation of a critical edge-device vulnerability, a large education-sector data breach tied to ShinyHunters, and major ecosystem-level changes intended to reduce JavaScript supply chain risk. The reporting also includes Windows platform reliability...
item.content:encoded: This digest window is dominated by active exploitation of a critical edge-device vulnerability, a large education-sector data breach tied to ShinyHunters, and major ecosystem-level changes intended to reduce JavaScript supply chain risk. The reporting also includes Windows platform reliability updates affecting BitLocker recovery behavior and continued attention to operational technology visibility gaps. Attackers are reportedly targeting CVE-2026-10520, a maximum-severity OS command injection flaw in Ivanti Sentry that enables code execution with root privileges on Internet-exposed secure mobile gateways. Ivanti patched the vulnerability in Sentry versions R10.5.2, R10.6.2, and R10.7.1, but Shadowserver reported soon after that exposed vulnerable systems had already been backdoored in some cases. Shadowserver observed 19 vulnerable Ivanti Sentry instances during scanning and said at least two were backdoored, warning that unpatched exposed systems were likely compromised. The report adds to a broader Ivanti risk pattern: CISA has flagged 34 vulnerabilities across Ivanti products as actively exploited in the wild, including 12 that were also targeted in ransomware attacks. The University of Nottingham confirmed unauthorized access to its student records system, affecting current students and alumni. Have I Been Pwned reported that 454,600 individuals were impacted, with exposed data including email addresses, names, addresses, phone numbers, ethnicities, disabilities, passport numbers, academic enrolment information, and fee payment information. The ShinyHunters extortion gang claimed responsibility and said it stole more than 40GB of documents from the University of Nottingham and its Malaysia and China campuses. The university said it is working with the third party that maintains the affected platform on a forensic investigation and has reported the incident to Action Fraud and the UK Information Commissioner’s Office. BleepingComputer linked the intrusion to a wider ShinyHunters campaign targeting cloud and on-premises Oracle PeopleSoft instances at more than 100 organizations globally. The incident highlights continued actor interest in enterprise student information systems and third-party-maintained platforms containing high-volume personal and administrative records. GitHub announced breaking changes planned for npm version 12 that will disable dependency install scripts by default. The change targets a long-standing supply chain risk in which npm install can execute preinstall, install, and postinstall scripts from transitive dependencies, allowing a compromised package anywhere in the dependency tree to run arbitrary code on developer systems or CI runners. Under the npm 12 model, dependency install scripts will not execute unless explicitly approved at the project level. npm install will also stop resolving Git dependencies or dependencies from remote URLs unless those behaviors are explicitly allowed with relevant options, narrowing default execution and resolution pathways commonly abused in dependency-chain compromise scenarios. Microsoft also resolved a Windows platform issue that caused some Windows Server 2025 systems to boot into BitLocker recovery after the April 2026 security update. The bug affected devices with BitLocker enabled on the OS drive and a specific unrecommended Group Policy configuration involving TPM platform validation and PCR7; fixes were released in KB5094125 for Windows Server 2025 and KB5093998 for Windows 11 23H2. A webinar titled “Closing the OT Security Visibility Gap: From Blind Spots to Continuous Protection” focuses on persistent visibility limitations in operational technology environments. The framing reflects a broader security concern around insufficient asset, traffic, and behavioral visibility across industrial networks where continuous monitoring remains harder than in conventional IT environments. The topic aligns with ongoing pressure on defenders to understand OT exposure and detect changes in environments that often include legacy systems, limited instrumentation, and operational constraints. While the article is webinar-focused rather than incident-driven, it reflects continued industry emphasis on closing monitoring gaps in critical infrastructure and industrial settings.

item.title: MCP Security Tradeoffs for Agentic SOC Access
item.description: This digest centers on the operational and security implications of Model Context Protocol (MCP) adoption in security environments. The reporting frames MCP not as a replacement for APIs, but as a parallel access model designed for AI agents and analyst-driven investigation workflows, with new...
item.content:encoded: This digest centers on the operational and security implications of Model Context Protocol (MCP) adoption in security environments. The reporting frames MCP not as a replacement for APIs, but as a parallel access model designed for AI agents and analyst-driven investigation workflows, with new implications for credential exposure and access control. Mitiga’s analysis distinguishes APIs and MCP by their intended consumers. APIs are described as contracts for code, best suited to deterministic automation where developers know which endpoints to call and require predictable behavior. MCP, by contrast, is presented as a contract for AI agents that need to discover, select, and chain tools during live investigative tasks. The article positions APIs as better aligned with scripted workflows, integrations, and repeatable automation. MCP is framed as more useful for analysts, AI assistants, and Agentic SOC platforms operating through natural language prompts, where the agent determines which tools to use and how to combine outputs during an investigation. A key reported benefit of MCP is reduced time-to-first-answer in security investigations. The article states that MCP can allow an LLM-driven system to choose tools, chain results, and format outputs without requiring a pre-built workflow, potentially compressing early investigative steps into minutes. The same access model also expands the security surface of AI-enabled SOC operations. Mitiga notes that MCP servers provide agents with credentialed access into security environments, making MCP tokens, scopes, and server permissions part of the effective attack surface. In this framing, MCP adoption introduces identity and authorization considerations similar to API security, but applied to autonomous or semi-autonomous agent activity. The dominant theme is the coexistence of deterministic automation and agentic investigation models. APIs remain central for predictable machine-to-machine execution, while MCP is presented as an emerging interface layer for AI-assisted security operations that can accelerate analysis while concentrating privileged access paths around agent tooling.

item.title: Langflow CVE-2026-5027 Exploitation, Miasma Leak, and PeopleSoft ShinyHunters Attacks
item.description: This digest window was dominated by active exploitation, developer ecosystem compromise, and policy movement around faster remediation of high-risk vulnerabilities. Reporting highlighted attacks against Langflow and Oracle PeopleSoft, continued evolution of self-propagating supply-chain malware,...
item.content:encoded: This digest window was dominated by active exploitation, developer ecosystem compromise, and policy movement around faster remediation of high-risk vulnerabilities. Reporting highlighted attacks against Langflow and Oracle PeopleSoft, continued evolution of self-propagating supply-chain malware, and defensive changes planned for npm in response to recent package ecosystem abuse. Attackers are actively exploiting CVE-2026-5027, a high-severity path traversal flaw in Langflow that allows arbitrary file writes on exposed servers. The issue affects the POST /api/v2/files endpoint, where the filename parameter in multipart form data is not properly sanitized. Tenable disclosed the vulnerability on March 27, 2026, after reporting it to Langflow more than two months earlier without receiving a response; Snyk reported fixes in langflow-base 0.8.3 and Langflow 1.9.0, with 1.10.0 listed as the latest release. Separately, ShinyHunters claimed responsibility for ongoing data theft attacks against Oracle PeopleSoft cloud and on-premises customer instances. The group claimed to have stolen data from 300 PeopleSoft instances across more than 100 organizations, with education reportedly making up most of the affected sector. The actor described its exploitation path as a “gadget chain” involving old and zero-day vulnerabilities, with impact dependent on instance configuration. Researcher Michael R identified exposed online directories containing apparent attack tooling related to the PeopleSoft activity, including MeshCentral agents, a defacement and credential spray script, and staging materials. The two cases underscore continued attacker focus on externally reachable enterprise platforms where file write, deserialization, or configuration-dependent exploit chains can quickly become data theft or persistence opportunities. The Miasma credential-stealing framework was briefly exposed through GitHub repositories named “Miasma-Open-Source-Release” across numerous compromised developer accounts. SafeDep assessed that Miasma appears to be an evolution of the earlier Shai-Hulud worm, sharing many features, techniques, and code. The framework steals build environment and cloud credentials, compromises legitimate repositories and packages, and publishes trojanized versions to reach downstream developers. A notable feature of Miasma is its use of GitHub itself for command-and-control-like functions, reducing dependence on separate infrastructure. The leaked code also revealed a dead-man switch capable of deleting files in a user’s home and Documents folders if a stolen GitHub token used for exfiltration is revoked. This behavior combines credential theft, repository abuse, and destructive pressure against victims attempting to cut off attacker access. GitHub announced npm v12 security changes intended to disrupt similar supply-chain tactics. In npm v12, npm install will not run dependency preinstall, install, or postinstall scripts unless explicitly approved, and it will no longer fetch dependencies from Git repositories or remote URLs such as HTTPS tarballs unless permitted. GitHub said the defaults could have disrupted techniques seen in malicious install scripts, data-stealing npm packages, and Git dependency abuse documented in Shai-Hulud attacks. JFrog’s discussion of IDC’s 2026 Software Engineering Challenges Report framed Shadow AI as a growing unmanaged enterprise risk, replacing Shadow IT as developers use personal accounts, unvetted plugins, and AI-generated code in production workflows. The report, based on a survey of 1,000 enterprise and mid-sized organizations, described a widening gap between AI adoption goals and governance readiness. The article argued that AI agents cannot be assumed to self-police compliance because their behavior is non-deterministic and may bypass policies that exist only as documentation or informal agreements. Aikido described a parallel shift in where code is written, citing PostHog’s observation that roughly as many pull requests are being merged through Slack as through the code editor. The article said developers increasingly write code through AI agents, chat interfaces, and MCP servers that may be outside security team visibility. It connected this dispersion to recent supply-chain incidents such as TeamPCP activity, the Axios maintainer account hijacking, and the Glassworm attack, which emphasized compromise of developer devices and accounts rather than application vulnerabilities. Developer endpoints were characterized as high-value control points because they often hold cloud credentials, SSH keys, npm publish tokens, Kubernetes configs, source code access, and production access. The reporting also noted limitations in traditional MDM and EDR visibility for malicious packages, IDE extensions, browser plugins, and postinstall hooks before credential theft occurs. CISA issued a new binding operational directive requiring U.S. federal civilian agencies to patch certain high-risk vulnerabilities within 72 hours. The accelerated timeline applies when vulnerabilities meet three of four criteria: public internet exposure, presence in the Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog, automated exploitability, and the degree of adversary control over the vulnerable system. Agencies have 180 days to adopt the new time frame. The directive also requires agencies to assess when and how a vulnerable system may have been compromised before patching, reflecting CISA’s position that applying a patch does not generally evict an existing threat actor. CISA also urged state, tribal, and local governments, along with critical infrastructure owners and operators, to adopt similar practices.

item.title: JDY Botnet, Langflow CVE-2026-5027 Exploitation, and Critical Ivanti/Fortinet/SAP Patches
item.description: This coverage window was dominated by AI-adjacent security risk, active exploitation of exposed software, and renewed attention on edge and third-party access. Reporting also highlighted a China-linked reconnaissance botnet expanding across SOHO and IoT infrastructure, alongside a disruptive...
item.content:encoded: This coverage window was dominated by AI-adjacent security risk, active exploitation of exposed software, and renewed attention on edge and third-party access. Reporting also highlighted a China-linked reconnaissance botnet expanding across SOHO and IoT infrastructure, alongside a disruptive cyberattack affecting Australian sugar production. Multiple articles focused on how AI agents challenge existing zero trust and security inspection models. Varonis and Netskope both described AI agents as non-human actors that authenticate, call APIs, access databases, invoke tools, and exchange data at machine speed, creating gaps in policies designed around human access and authentication-time controls. Reported threat scenarios included prompt injection, tool poisoning, identity and privilege abuse, memory poisoning, supply chain compromise, and uninspected Model Context Protocol or app-to-LLM traffic. Software supply chain exposure in AI-assisted development also featured prominently. Socket announced an integration with Replit intended to evaluate open source packages as they enter AI-powered build workflows, citing threats such as typosquatting, impersonated packages, malicious transitive dependencies, credential stealers, and install scripts that retrieve second-stage payloads. The reporting reflects a broader shift in which AI agents can recommend, install, and wire dependencies directly into applications. SpecterOps published research on SQL Server 2025 AI-related features, demonstrating offensive use cases involving spinvokeexternalrestendpoint, CREATE EXTERNAL MODEL, and AIGENERATEEMBEDDINGS. The proof-of-concept activity included HTTPS exfiltration of table contents and files, trigger-based persistence for newly inserted data, NTLM coercion over SMB via UNC paths for ONNX models, and command-and-control patterns disguised as embedding-style traffic. Langflow CVE-2026-5027 stood out because it is reported as unpatched and under active exploitation. Tenable described the flaw as a path traversal issue in the POST /api/v2/files endpoint caused by insufficient sanitization of multipart filename values, while VulnCheck reported that default unauthenticated auto-login can allow attackers to obtain a session token and reach remote code execution. Censys data cited in the reporting identified roughly 7,000 internet-exposed Langflow instances, with most in North America. Patch reporting covered several critical enterprise products. Fortinet fixed CVE-2026-25089, a CVSS 9.1 command injection flaw in FortiSandbox products; Ivanti fixed CVE-2026-10520, a CVSS 10.0 unauthenticated root-level command injection issue in Ivanti Sentry, and CVE-2026-10523, a CVSS 9.9 authentication bypass enabling creation of arbitrary administrative accounts. SAP also released fixes for critical vulnerabilities affecting NetWeaver AS ABAP and ABAP Platform, SAP Commerce Cloud, SAP Data Hub, and NetWeaver Application Server Java, with no exploitation reported for those listed issues. CISA added Cisco, Chrome, and Arista vulnerabilities to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog. The additions included CVE-2026-20245 in Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Manager, CVE-2026-11645 in Google Chrome V8, and CVE-2026-7473 in Arista EOS. Arista stated it does not plan to patch CVE-2026-7473 due to potential configuration breakage and instead documented ACL-based mitigations. Black Lotus Labs reporting, covered by both The Hacker News and BleepingComputer, described the China-linked JDY botnet as having expanded to more than 1,500 compromised SOHO and IoT devices. The botnet is used as a centrally controlled reconnaissance network for service discovery, banner grabbing, TLS certificate collection, protocol fingerprinting, and flaw-focused scanning after public vulnerability disclosures. The botnet has expanded beyond earlier Cisco RV320 and RV325 router activity to include devices from Araknis, Mimosa Networks, Ubiquiti, DrayTek, Hikvision, Linksys, and Cisco. Researchers said JDY places strong emphasis on U.S. targets, including U.S. military and associated networks. Command infrastructure reportedly uses Tor hidden services, and some activity involves the open-source Platypus reverse-shell and host-management framework. A cyberattack disrupted Mackay Sugar operations in Queensland, forcing shutdowns at the Farleigh and Racecourse mills and prompting growers to stop harvesting sugarcane until further communication. The company said it engaged cybersecurity experts and local authorities, implemented temporary measures to keep essential operations running, and was working to restore affected systems. Mackay Sugar did not disclose whether ransomware, data theft, or another form of malicious activity was involved. Identity and access management remained a parallel theme, particularly around third parties and account verification. Health-ISAC released a white paper on third-party IAM risks in healthcare, emphasizing vendor, partner, remote access, connected device, and facility management complexity. Separate reporting on identity verification cited a 160% surge in credential theft in 2025 and described phishing-resistant MFA, service desk verification, device trust signals, and passkeys as major areas of focus in modern identity programs.

item.title: CVE-2026-42897, CVE-2026-45657, and AI-Enabled Phishing Drive June Threat Coverage
item.description: This digest window was dominated by Microsoft’s unusually large June security release, including an actively exploited Exchange Server zero-day and a critical Windows kernel issue described as wormable. Reporting also highlighted continued evolution in phishing and initial access tradecraft, with...
item.content:encoded: This digest window was dominated by Microsoft’s unusually large June security release, including an actively exploited Exchange Server zero-day and a critical Windows kernel issue described as wormable. Reporting also highlighted continued evolution in phishing and initial access tradecraft, with AI-generated sites, adversary-in-the-middle kits, event-themed fraud, and low-content email payloads complicating detection. Microsoft patched CVE-2026-42897, an actively exploited high-severity spoofing vulnerability in Exchange Server affecting Exchange Server 2016, Exchange Server 2019, and Exchange Server Subscription Edition. The flaw can be exploited remotely without privileges by sending a specially crafted email that, under certain Outlook Web Access interaction conditions, executes arbitrary JavaScript in the user’s browser context. CISA added the vulnerability to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog on May 15 and set a May 29 remediation deadline for U.S. federal agencies. Microsoft’s June Patch Tuesday fixed more than 200 security flaws, described by multiple vulnerability trackers as the largest monthly release in the program’s history. Among the most severe was CVE-2026-45657, a critical Windows kernel vulnerability rated 9.8 that could allow remote full system compromise without user interaction and was characterized by ZDI as wormable. The release also included CVE-2026-41091, an actively exploited Microsoft Defender elevation-of-privilege flaw that could allow an attacker with an existing foothold to gain full control of a system. The same Microsoft coverage included three disclosed zero-days, including CVE-2026-50507, a BitLocker bypass tied to the pseudonymous researcher Nightmare Eclipse. Separately, Microsoft reported that a small percentage of devices upgraded to Windows 11 versions 24H2 or 25H2 may fail to install the latest cumulative update, with errors including 0x80073712 and 0x800f0993 and logs referencing component store corruption or missing hydration candidates. Zscaler ThreatLabz reported that phishing activity declined by roughly 20% year over year in both 2024 and 2025, while attackers shifted toward more targeted and personalized lures resembling routine business workflows. The report identified 413,524 AI-generated site instances, with 37,447, or 9.06%, flagged as malicious, and attributed builders including Manus AI, Blackbox AI, and Anything AI. ThreatLabz also found that 95.2% of phishing activity was delivered over encrypted channels. The same research described phishing kits using adversary-in-the-middle and browser-in-the-middle techniques to capture credentials and MFA codes during live login flows, enabling session-level compromise rather than simple password theft. ThreatLabz also recorded 89.9 million hostile interactions with external decoys over six months and observed more than 121,000 distinct AWS-hosted IPs probing customer environments, underscoring the scale of opportunistic reconnaissance against exposed infrastructure. Event-themed fraud is already appearing around the FIFA World Cup 2026. The FBI warned that threat actors are creating fraudulent FIFA-affiliated websites to collect personal data, conduct financial fraud, and sell fake goods or services, while Cyble confirmed that many FBI-flagged domains remained active at publication time. Reported examples included ww-fifa[.]com, a fake hospitality and ticket portal requesting personal and payment details, and fifaworldcup-careers[.]com, a fake recruitment domain associated with multiple IP addresses and wildcard SSL certificates. IRONSCALES analyzed an “ACH payment” email sent from carlaburbo@keystonenatural[.]com to an accounts receivable team, with ap@keystonenatural[.]com copied. The message passed SPF, DKIM, DMARC, and composite authentication at final delivery, while original authentication results showed dkim=none and dmarc=none, and ARC validation at i=2 returned cv=fail, indicating a broken forwarding chain of trust. The email’s payload consisted entirely of a single PNG attachment named image002.png, with no message body, no links, and no OCR-extracted text. Themis flagged the message at 54% confidence as credential theft, and one mailbox was quarantined, illustrating how image-only payloads and authentication-chain inconsistencies can coexist with apparently successful final-delivery authentication outcomes. A related control-validation theme appeared in reporting on automated pentesting limitations. The article argued that clean automated pentest results can create false confidence because they show exploitable paths but not whether SIEM rules fired, EDR alerts were raised, or SOC analysts had sufficient signal to act. It contrasted automated pentesting with breach and attack simulation, framing the latter as focused on whether controls blocked, detected, logged, or missed known malicious behaviors.

item.title: Microsoft YellowKey, phpBB RCE, ServiceNow Exploitation, and Ivanti Sentry Root RCE
item.description: This coverage window is dominated by high-impact vulnerability disclosures affecting widely deployed enterprise and community platforms, including Windows, phpBB, ServiceNow, and Ivanti Sentry. Several items involve either confirmed exploitation, public proof-of-concept activity, or flaws that...
item.content:encoded: This coverage window is dominated by high-impact vulnerability disclosures affecting widely deployed enterprise and community platforms, including Windows, phpBB, ServiceNow, and Ivanti Sentry. Several items involve either confirmed exploitation, public proof-of-concept activity, or flaws that enable full system or administrative compromise. A parallel theme is the increasing role of advanced AI systems in vulnerability discovery and the controls vendors are placing around cyber-capable models. Microsoft’s June 2026 Patch Tuesday addressed three zero-day vulnerabilities known as GreenPlasma, MiniPlasma, and YellowKey. GreenPlasma and MiniPlasma are local privilege escalation flaws in the Collaborative Translation Framework and Cloud Files Mini Filter Driver, respectively, and can allow attackers to obtain SYSTEM privileges on fully patched Windows systems prior to remediation. YellowKey is a Windows Recovery Environment backdoor affecting unpatched Windows 11 and Windows Server 2022/2025 systems, enabling attackers with physical access to bypass BitLocker protections. The vulnerabilities were disclosed by a researcher using the handle Nightmare Eclipse, who framed the release as a protest against Microsoft Security Response Center disclosure practices. The same researcher has reportedly released proof-of-concept exploits for additional Windows zero-days, including BlueHammer, RedSun, UnDefend, and RoguePlanet. This creates a broader exposure pattern in which defenders face not only patched vulnerabilities, but also an expanding set of public exploit artifacts tied to unresolved or recently disclosed Windows flaws. Aikido reported a critical phpBB authentication bypass vulnerability that can lead to remote code execution and complete takeover of the underlying system. The flaw affects phpBB 3.3.16 and below, as well as 4.0.0-a2, and is exploitable in the default configuration with a single unauthenticated HTTP request to obtain a valid session as any user. phpBB released version 3.3.17 after the issue was reported through HackerOne on June 2, with a working patch published on June 6. The update also introduces a breaking change for OAuth authentication users because the redirect URI handler has moved to /user/oauth/authenticate/..., which may affect deployments relying on existing OAuth flows. Ivanti also disclosed two critical Sentry secure mobile gateway vulnerabilities. CVE-2026-10520 is a maximum-severity OS command injection flaw allowing remote code execution as root, while CVE-2026-10523 is an authentication bypass that can allow unauthenticated remote attackers to create rogue administrative accounts and gain full administrative access; Ivanti stated it had no evidence of exploitation at disclosure time. ServiceNow reported that unknown threat actors exploited a security issue to gain greater unauthorized access to susceptible customer instances than intended. The issue does not currently have a CVE identifier, and details first emerged publicly through Reddit before ServiceNow described anomalous activity and successful queries of instance tables affecting a subset of customers. ServiceNow applied a hosted-instance security update on June 5, changing an endpoint configuration to restrict access to authenticated users. The affected population includes customers on the Australia platform release and customers that made certain configuration changes to instances running releases prior to Australia. The incident is notable because it involves exploitation against a major enterprise workflow platform and successful access to customer instance data. ServiceNow said affected customers were notified after evidence of successful table queries was observed. Anthropic released Claude Fable 5 publicly while limiting its cyber-unrestricted counterpart, Claude Mythos 5, to vetted cyber defenders and critical infrastructure operators. The company said Mythos-class models can find and exploit software vulnerabilities at a level where unrestricted public access would provide attackers with significant uplift. Claude Fable 5 uses safety classifiers that route flagged cyber, biology, chemistry, and distillation requests to the weaker Claude Opus 4.8 rather than refusing them outright. Anthropic also said that during Project Glasswing, it and roughly 50 partners used Claude Mythos Preview to identify more than ten thousand high- or critical-severity vulnerabilities in systemically important software. Anthropic will require 30-day data retention for traffic on Fable 5, Mythos 5, and future models at this capability level, while stating that retained data will not be used for training or non-safety purposes. The release reflects a widening gap between public AI capabilities and restricted cyber-specialized systems intended for controlled defensive use.
